lede: A blockchain is just a database that is shared across a network of computers. But instead of trusting a single company (like a bank) to keep the records safe, the network uses cryptography and consensus rules to make the data virtually impossible to change or hack.

case1.title: The Genesis Block
case1.desc: Every blockchain starts with a Genesis Block (Block 0). It has some data and a unique cryptographic Hash (its digital fingerprint). Because it's the first block, its 'Previous Hash' is just 0000.

case1.title: A New Transaction Arrives
case1.desc: Someone wants to send money. The transaction is grouped into Block 1. But before it can be added to the chain, it must be 'mined'.

case1.title: Mining (Proof of Work)
case1.desc: Miners compete to find a special number (Nonce) that, when combined with the block's data, produces a Hash that starts with a certain number of zeroes. This takes immense computing power.

case1.title: Block Chained
case1.desc: A miner finds the correct Nonce! Block 1 is added. Crucially, Block 1 includes the Hash of Block 0. This 'link' is what makes it a chain.

case2.title: A Healthy Chain
case2.desc: Here we have a perfectly healthy chain of 3 blocks. Notice how Block 2's 'Previous Hash' exactly matches Block 1's 'Hash'. Everything is mathematically linked.

case2.title: The Hack Attempt
case2.desc: A hacker tries to change the data in Block 1 (e.g., changing '$10' to '$1000'). Press the 'TAMPER' button on Block 1 to simulate the attack.

case2.title: The Chain Breaks
case2.desc: Because the data in Block 1 changed, its Hash completely changed. Now, Block 2's 'Previous Hash' no longer matches Block 1. Block 2 and Block 3 are instantly invalidated (red). To get away with this, the hacker would have to re-mine Block 1, 2, and 3 before anyone else adds a new block—which is practically impossible.

case3.title: Nodes in Sync
case3.desc: A blockchain is a Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network. Here, Node A, Node B, and Node C all have the exact same copy of the blockchain.

case3.title: A Temporary Fork
case3.desc: Sometimes, two miners solve a block at the exact same time! Node A receives Block X, but Node C receives Block Y. The network is temporarily split (a Fork).

case3.title: The Longest Chain Wins
case3.desc: The tie is broken when the NEXT block is found. A miner finds Block Z and attaches it to Node A's chain. The rule of blockchain is 'The Longest Chain is the Truth'. Node C sees Node A's chain is longer, drops Block Y, and copies Node A's chain to regain consensus.
